intent: Build the cross-runtime parity harness — same Python source through Form-native python-bmf.fk and through the emitted Python compiler, diff token streams. Begin the comparison loop the user named: 'compare the language native execution to the form native execution, optimize the kernel, the compiler, the emmitor.'

what_changed.form_side_scan_driver: form/form-stdlib/emits/python-bmf-scan-driver.fk — Form recipe that calls python-source-scan-file and writes tokens as kind\tvalue lines
what_changed.parity_harness: form/scripts/cross_runtime_parity.sh — invokes form-kernel-go with core+source-compiler+engine+compiler+python-bmf+driver, then runs emitted Python scanner on the same file, diffs the outputs
what_changed.cross_runtime_test: kernels/python_bmf/tests/test_cross_runtime.py — automates parity check across all parity-suite demos
what_changed.known_gaps_updated: kernels/python_bmf/KNOWN_GAPS.md — adds Cross-runtime parity section with the table of which workloads agree and which diverge, naming each divergence as a next-breath candidate

parity_results.emitted_other_modules: diverge — mostly because Python side has features (string escape preservation, quote-style tagging, triple-quoted variants) Form side doesn't yet have

key_finding: At the scanner level, both runtimes agree on every token category and value for code within the python-bmf.fk feature surface. Divergences point in ONE direction: the emitted Python scanner has more features than the Form-native scanner currently exposes. These are not bugs — they're the next-breath candidates for extending python-bmf.fk's `python-source-scan-string-loop` to preserve backslash escapes, tag quote variants, etc. Bringing those into the Form side closes the loop.

proof.result: Form-side tokens: 218 lines; Python tokens: 218 lines; Diff: PARITY — both runtimes produce identical token streams.

next_breaths: Extend python-bmf.fk scanner to preserve backslash escapes — close one divergence direction.
Add reverse actions to BMF rules so the rule itself supplies its inverse for decompilation.

Build a Form-side rule-application driver that takes Python file → applies python-bmf-rules → writes .fkb. Compare .fkb output between Form-native and emitted-Python compilers — the deeper proof loop.
Add performance harness: time-per-iter and peak RSS for both runtimes scanning + rule-applying the same workload.
